Name: pac_bayes_latency
Title: PAC-Bayes Frameworks for Latency-Aware Neural Network Optimization
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, we can develop novel latency-aware optimization methods for neural networks, achieving a better trade-off between accuracy and inference time.
Related Work: Recent studies, such as SNIP and EMQ, have explored adaptive mixed-precision training frameworks and evolving training-free proxies for automated mixed precision quantization. However, these approaches do not explicitly address latency-aware optimization. Our proposal builds upon the concept of latency-aware inference and integrates PAC-Bayes frameworks to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference.
Abstract: This research proposes a novel approach to latency-aware optimization of neural networks using PAC-Bayes frameworks. By leveraging spectral stability bounds, we aim to develop methods that can efficiently optimize neural networks for both accuracy and inference time. Our approach integrates a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework to minimize hidden-state perturbation and enforces strict wall-clock budgets. Experimental evaluations will focus on latency benchmarks with explicit perturbation-cost and memory-bandwidth accounting, targeting throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers.
Experiments: Apply the proposed latency-aware optimization method to various neural network architectures (e.g., CNN, RNN, Transformer) and benchmarking datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10, MNIST).
Evaluate the performance of our method using metrics such as inference time, accuracy, and stability.
Compare the results with state-of-the-art optimization methods to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of integrating PAC-Bayes frameworks with spectral stability bounds may require significant computational resources.
The applicability of our method to different types of neural networks and datasets may be limited.
Potential overfitting or underfitting issues due to the latency-aware optimization process.
